violations.message: [avoid_static_state] Static mutable state persists across hot-reloads and tests, causing stale data and inconsistent behavior. Tests fail unpredictably due to shared state leaking between runs, and production bugs become hard to reproduce across different app sessions and isolates. {v4}
violations.correction: Replace static mutable fields with scoped state management (Provider, Riverpod, or Bloc) to ensure proper isolation across tests and hot-reloads.

violations.message: [avoid_similar_names] Variable name differs from another in-scope variable by only one or two characters. Near-identical names increase the risk of accidentally using the wrong variable, producing subtle bugs that pass code review because the names look correct at a glance. {v4}
violations.correction: Rename one or both variables to be more distinct, using descriptive names that clearly convey their different purposes (e.g. userInput vs validatedInput).

violations.message: [prefer_static_method] Method does not reference any instance members and could be static. Non-static methods that ignore instance state mislead readers into thinking the method depends on object state, and they prevent calling the method without an instance. {v4}
violations.correction: Mark the method as static to communicate that it operates independently of instance state and can be called without constructing the class.

violations.message: [missing_use_result_annotation] Function returns a value without @useResult annotation. Callers may accidentally ignore the return value, leading to missed error handling, lost data transformations, or incorrectly assuming the function has side effects when it does not. {v5}
violations.correction: Add @useResult annotation above the function declaration to signal that the returned value must be used. Include a reason parameter explaining what happens if the value is ignored.

violations.message: [move_variable_closer_to_its_usage] Variable declared far from its first use, with many unrelated statements in between. This forces readers to hold the variable in memory while reading irrelevant code, reducing comprehension and increasing the risk of accidental reuse or shadowing. {v7}
violations.correction: Move the variable declaration to just before its first usage. This narrows the scope, improves readability, and makes the data flow easier to follow.

violations.message: [avoid_excessive_expressions] Expression has excessive complexity (>5 operators). Complex expressions are hard to read and maintain. This excessive complexity makes the code harder to understand, test, and maintain. {v4}
violations.correction: Break into smaller expressions with named variables. Verify the change works correctly with existing tests and add coverage for the new behavior.

violations.message: [avoid_unnecessary_nullable_return_type] Return type is nullable but function never returns null. Unnecessary nullability forces callers to add redundant null checks, reducing code clarity and type safety. {v3}
violations.correction: Remove the ? from the return type. Verify the change works correctly with existing tests and add coverage for the new behavior.

violations.message: [avoid_nested_conditional_expressions] Ternary expression contains another ternary, creating a multi-level conditional that is difficult to parse visually. Nested ternaries lack clear precedence at a glance and are a common source of logic errors during editing. {v4}
violations.correction: Refactor to an if-else statement for clarity, or extract the inner conditional into a named variable or helper method with a descriptive name.

violations.message: [avoid_nested_shorthands] Deeply nested shorthand syntax (e.g., cascades inside ternaries inside null-aware operators) reduces readability and makes expressions difficult to understand at a glance. Each nesting level multiplies cognitive load, increasing the risk of logic errors during code review and maintenance. {v5}
violations.correction: Extract nested expressions into named local variables or helper methods. Each intermediate value should have a descriptive name that communicates its purpose, making the overall logic easier to follow, test, and debug.

violations.message: [prefer_static_class] Class contains only static members and acts as a namespace. Static-only classes cannot be instantiated meaningfully, add unnecessary boilerplate, and prevent tree-shaking of unused members in the class. {v5}
violations.correction: Replace with top-level functions and constants, which are simpler, support tree-shaking, and follow idiomatic Dart conventions.

violations.message: [missing_use_result_annotation] Function returns a value without @useResult annotation. Callers may accidentally ignore the return value, leading to missed error handling, lost data transformations, or incorrectly assuming the function has side effects when it does not. {v5}

violations.message: [prefer_switch_expression] Switch statement with only return/assignment detected. Using statement syntax for simple value mapping adds unnecessary boilerplate (break statements, case keywords) and makes the code more verbose and harder to scan. {v5}
violations.correction: Replace with a switch expression that directly produces the mapped value. Switch expressions are more concise, cannot forget break statements, and guarantee exhaustiveness checking, reducing bugs and improving readability.

violations.message: [move_variable_closer_to_its_usage] Variable declared far from its first use, with many unrelated statements in between. This forces readers to hold the variable in memory while reading irrelevant code, reducing comprehension and increasing the risk of accidental reuse or shadowing. {v7}

violations.message: [avoid_redundant_else] Else clause follows a branch that already exits via return, throw, break, or continue. The else keyword is redundant because the code after the if block only runs when the condition is false, adding unnecessary nesting and indentation. {v3}
violations.correction: Remove the else clause and un-indent the code so it runs at the same level as the if block, reducing nesting depth and improving readability.

violations.message: [avoid_excessive_expressions] Expression has excessive complexity (>5 operators). Complex expressions are hard to read and maintain. This excessive complexity makes the code harder to understand, test, and maintain. {v4}
violations.correction: Break into smaller expressions with named variables. Verify the change works correctly with existing tests and add coverage for the new behavior.

violations.message: [avoid_duplicate_number_elements] Duplicate numeric element in collection literal typically indicates a copy-paste error or logic mistake. In Sets, the duplicate is silently ignored, producing a smaller collection than expected. {v2}
violations.correction: Remove the duplicate numeric element. If intentional (e.g., days-in-month arrays), suppress with // ignore.

violations.message: [avoid_long_parameter_list] Function has too many parameters (max 5). Functions with many parameters are difficult to call correctly, hard to test with all combinations, and indicate the function may be doing too much work. {v6}
violations.correction: Group related parameters into a configuration object or convert positional parameters to named parameters for self-documenting call sites.

violations.message: [avoid_unnecessary_if] If statement returns true in one branch and false in the other, which is equivalent to returning the condition expression directly. The extra branching adds visual complexity without changing the result. {v4}
violations.correction: Replace the if/else with a direct return of the condition (e.g. return condition;) or its negation (return !condition;) for clarity.

violations.message: [avoid_barrel_files] File contains only export statements (barrel file). Barrel files increase build times by pulling in unused code and obscure dependency tracking, making it harder to identify which modules depend on which implementations. {v6}
violations.correction: Import specific files where needed instead of using barrel files. Direct imports make dependency graphs explicit and enable tree-shaking.

violations.message: [require_test_setup_teardown] Test file lacks setUp/tearDown for shared resources. Tests should properly set up and clean up resources. This weakens test quality, making failures harder to diagnose and reducing confidence in the test suite. {v5}
violations.correction: Add setUp() and tearDown() to initialize and clean up test state. Run the full test suite to confirm the refactored tests maintain equivalent coverage.

violations.message: [avoid_misused_test_matchers] Raw literal used as a test matcher instead of a proper matcher function. Using literals (true, false, null) as the second argument to expect() produces poor failure messages that show "Expected: true, Actual: false" instead of descriptive matcher output. Proper matchers like isTrue, isFalse, isNull, hasLength, equals, and isA provide clearer assertion semantics and significantly better diagnostic output when tests fail, making debugging faster. {v1}
violations.correction: Replace literal matchers with proper test matchers: expect(x, true) => expect(x, isTrue), expect(x, false) => expect(x, isFalse), expect(x, null) => expect(x, isNull), expect(list.length, N) => expect(list, hasLength(N)).

violations.message: [prefer_matcher_over_equals] Use matchers instead of direct equality to improve messages. Matchers provide better error messages and more expressive tests. Expect uses equality instead of matchers. {v7}
violations.correction: Replace with isTrue, isFalse, isNull, hasLength(), etc. Run the full test suite to confirm the refactored tests maintain equivalent coverage.

Proper matchers like isTrue, isFalse, isNull, hasLength, equals, and isA provide clearer assertion semantics and significantly better diagnostic output when tests fail, making debugging faster. {v1}
violations.correction: Replace literal matchers with proper test matchers: expect(x, true) => expect(x, isTrue), expect(x, false) => expect(x, isFalse), expect(x, null) => expect(x, isNull), expect(list.length, N) => expect(list, hasLength(N)).

violations.correction: Replace with isTrue, isFalse, isNull, hasLength(), etc. Run the full test suite to confirm the refactored tests maintain equivalent coverage.

violations.message: [avoid_misused_test_matchers] Raw literal used as a test matcher instead of a proper matcher function. Using literals (true, false, null) as the second argument to expect() produces poor failure messages that show "Expected: true, Actual: false" instead of descriptive matcher output. Proper matchers like isTrue, isFalse, isNull, hasLength, equals, and isA provide clearer assertion semantics and significantly better diagnostic output when tests fail, making debugging faster. {v1}
violations.correction: Replace literal matchers with proper test matchers: expect(x, true) => expect(x, isTrue), expect(x, false) => expect(x, isFalse), expect(x, null) => expect(x, isNull), expect(list.length, N) => expect(list, hasLength(N)).

Proper matchers like isTrue, isFalse, isNull, hasLength, equals, and isA provide clearer assertion semantics and significantly better diagnostic output when tests fail, making debugging faster. {v1}
violations.correction: Replace literal matchers with proper test matchers: expect(x, true) => expect(x, isTrue), expect(x, false) => expect(x, isFalse), expect(x, null) => expect(x, isNull), expect(list.length, N) => expect(list, hasLength(N)).

violations.message: [prefer_matcher_over_equals] Use matchers instead of direct equality to improve messages. Matchers provide better error messages and more expressive tests. Expect uses equality instead of matchers. {v7}
violations.correction: Replace with isTrue, isFalse, isNull, hasLength(), etc. Run the full test suite to confirm the refactored tests maintain equivalent coverage.
